\begin{proposition}
\label{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial}
Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
function
$$
\mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
n \longmapsto [M_n]
$$
see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
then this function is a numerical polynomial.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
$M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
the induction hypothesis applies to the
graded ring $S/(x)$.

\medskip\noindent
First we show the result holds if $x$ is nilpotent on $M$.
This we do by induction on the minimal integer $r$ such that
$x^r M  = 0$. If $r = 1$, then $M$ is a module over $S/xS$
and the result holds (by the other induction hypothesis).
If $r > 1$, then we can find a short exact sequence
$0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0$ such that the integers
$r', r''$ are strictly smaller than $r$. Thus we know
the result for $M''$ and $M'$. Hence
we get the result for $M$ because of the relation
$
[M_d]  = [M'_d] + [M''_d]
$
in $K'_0(S_0)$.

\medskip\noindent
If $x$ is not nilpotent on $M$, let $M' \subset M$ be
the largest submodule on which $x$ is nilpotent.
Consider the exact sequence $0 \to M' \to M \to M/M' \to 0$
we see again it suffices to prove the result for $M/M'$. In other
words we may assume that multiplication by $x$ is injective.

\medskip\noindent
Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
following short exact sequence
$$
0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
$$
This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
\label{remark-period-polynomial}
If $S$ is still Noetherian but $S$ is not generated in degree $1$,
then the function associated to a graded $S$-module is a periodic
polynomial (i.e., it is a numerical polynomial on the
congruence classes of integers modulo $n$ for some $n$).
\end{remark}

\subsection{The graded-module wrapper for the Stacks~00K1 inductive step}
\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}

The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction: to apply the
inductive hypothesis to the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
element, those two modules must be presented as finitely generated graded modules
over the smaller ring \(R/(x)\). This subsection records the small wrapper that
manufactures that presentation. It is a thin layer over existing Mathlib scaffold:
Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the quotient \(M/xM\) as a named
object (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of homogeneous
elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib});
what is missing -- and supplied here -- is the \emph{induced grading} on the
derived objects (a homogeneous submodule, a homogeneous quotient module, the
quotient ring \(R/(x)\)) together with the regrading and finite-module transfer
over \(R/(x)\). Each block mirrors the shape of Mathlib's \(\mathrm{HomogeneousIdeal}\)
/ \(\mathrm{GradedRing}\) API: an unbundled \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\)
instance on a family \(n \mapsto (\cdot)\) of submodules, together with a
\(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\) instance for the action.

\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a
finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\).
Fix a homogeneous element \(x \in R_1\). Write \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\)
for the graded kernel of multiplication by \(x\) and \(C = M/xM\) for the cokernel.

\emph{Build order.} The blocks are most cheaply discharged in the order
\(G_1 \to G_2 \to G_5 \to G_3 \to G_4 \to\) assembly: \(G_1\), \(G_2\), \(G_5\)
carry the least risk and unblock the most, while the degreewise rank--nullity
identity \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) is pure linear algebra
and can be discharged independently of all the graded blocks.

\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}

\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
  \emph{homogeneous} (\(p.\mathrm{IsHomogeneous}\,\mathcal{M}\)) when it is closed
  under taking homogeneous components: \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece
  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a
  set. Mathlib also bundles this as \(\mathrm{HomogeneousSubmodule}\,\mathcal{A}\,
  \mathcal{M}\); it provides the \(\mathrm{SetLike}\)/order/extensionality scaffold
  but \emph{not} the induced grading on \(p\) itself
  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[The quotient module \(M/xM\)]
  \label{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}
  \lean{QuotSMulTop}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.QuotSMulTop}).}
  For a commutative ring \(R\), an element \(x \in R\) and an \(R\)-module \(M\),
  Mathlib names the quotient
  \[
    \mathrm{QuotSMulTop}\,x\,M \;=\; M \,\big/\, x \cdot (\top : \mathrm{Submodule}\,R\,M)
    \;=\; M/xM,
  \]
  the cokernel of multiplication by \(x\) on \(M\). It is the object \(C = M/xM\)
  whose induced grading is supplied by
  \cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
  is homogeneous; this is the input that makes \(R/(x)\) a graded ring
  (\cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
  \[
    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
  \]
  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
  surjection \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\), this transfers finite generation of
  \(C\) and \(K\) from \(R\) to \(R/(x)\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_finite_transfer}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
  via \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\). If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
  feeds the induced gradings \(G_1\)--\(G_4\).
\end{lemma}

\subsubsection*{Induced gradings on the derived objects}

\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ -- grading on a homogeneous submodule]
  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(R\)-module and let
  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
  \(n \mapsto p \cap M_n\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces carries a
  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) -- i.e. \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\)
  internally -- together with a \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\,\mathcal{A}\)
  instance for the \(R\)-action restricted to \(p\). In particular the graded
  kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\), which is a homogeneous submodule (an element
  \(m = \sum_n m_n\) satisfies \(x m = 0\) iff each \(x m_n = 0\), because the
  \(x m_n\) lie in distinct degrees), inherits the grading
  \(K_n = K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  The internal decomposition of \(M\) restricts to \(p\). For \(m \in p\), write
  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) (the
  decomposition of \(M\)); homogeneity of \(p\) gives \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\),
  hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p \cap M_n\). This exhibits every element of \(p\)
  as a finite sum of elements of the subspaces \(p \cap M_n\), so the canonical map
  \(\bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n) \to p\) is surjective; it is injective because the
  \(M_n\) are independent in \(M\). Thus the family is a
  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\). For the graded action: if \(a \in R_i\) and
  \(m \in p \cap M_n\), then \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by
  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} and \(a m \in p\) because \(p\)
  is a submodule, so \(a m \in p \cap M_{i+n}\), which is the
  \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\) condition. Finally \(K\) is homogeneous: the
  components \(x m_n \in M_{n+1}\) of \(x m\) lie in distinct degrees, so
  \(x m = 0\) forces each \(x m_n = 0\); thus \(K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to
  M_{n+1})\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[$G_2$ -- grading on the quotient by a homogeneous submodule]
  \label{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.quotient_decomposition}
  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule. Then the quotient module
  \(M/p\) carries a \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) with components the images
  \((M_n).\mathrm{map}\,(p.\mathrm{mkQ})\) of the graded pieces under the quotient
  map \(p.\mathrm{mkQ} : M \to M/p\), together with a compatible
  \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\,\mathcal{A}\) instance. Specialising to
  \(p = xM = x \cdot (\top)\) gives the induced grading on
  \(C = M/xM = \mathrm{QuotSMulTop}\,x\,M\)
  (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), with components
  \(C_{n} = (M_{n}).\mathrm{map}\,(\mathrm{mkQ})\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  Surjectivity of \(\bigoplus_n (M_n).\mathrm{map}\,(p.\mathrm{mkQ}) \to M/p\) is
  immediate: \(p.\mathrm{mkQ}\) is surjective and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\), so every
  class \([m]\) is a finite sum of classes \([m_n]\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). For
  independence, suppose \(\sum_n [m_n] = 0\) in \(M/p\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). Then
  \(\sum_n m_n \in p\); by \(G_1\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(p =
  \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\), so taking degree-\(n\) components gives
  \(m_n \in p \cap M_n\), i.e. \([m_n] = 0\) for every \(n\). Hence the components
  \((M_n).\mathrm{map}\,(p.\mathrm{mkQ})\) are independent and the family is a
  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) of \(M/p\). Concretely this realises
  \(M/p \cong \bigoplus_n M_n/(p \cap M_n)\). The graded action descends: for
  \(a \in R_i\) and a class \([m]\) with \(m \in M_n\), \(a \cdot [m] = [a m]\) and
  \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, so the
  action lands in component \(i+n\); this is the \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\)
  instance. Taking \(p = xM\) -- homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) part
  \(x \cdot M_n\) by \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} -- yields the
  grading on \(C = \mathrm{QuotSMulTop}\,x\,M\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[$G_3$ -- the quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is a graded ring]
  \label{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.quotientRing_gradedRing}
  \uses{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib, lem:graded_quotient_decomposition,
    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  Let \(x \in R_1\), so that the principal ideal \((x)\) is homogeneous
  (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}). Then the quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is
  a graded ring with degree-\(n\) component the image
  \((R_n).\mathrm{map}\,(\mathrm{Ideal.Quotient.mk}\,(x))\) of \(R_n\) under the
  quotient ring homomorphism; that is, \(R/(x) = \bigoplus_n
  R_n/(R_n \cap (x))\) is a \(\mathrm{GradedRing}\) for this family.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib, lem:graded_quotient_decomposition,
    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
  Viewing \(R\) as a graded module over itself and the homogeneous ideal \((x)\) as
  a homogeneous submodule, \(G_2\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}) supplies a
  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) of the underlying additive group of
  \(R/(x)\) with components \((R_n).\mathrm{map}\,(\mathrm{Ideal.Quotient.mk}\,(x))\).
  It remains to check the multiplicative compatibility making this a
  \(\mathrm{GradedRing}\): the unit \(1\) is the image of \(1 \in R_0\), hence lies
  in the degree-zero component, and for \(\bar a \in (R/(x))_i\),
  \(\bar b \in (R/(x))_j\) -- images of \(a \in R_i\), \(b \in R_j\) -- the product
  \(\bar a \, \bar b = \overline{ab}\) is the image of \(a b \in R_{i+j}\) (because
  the homogeneous multiplication of \(R\) satisfies \(R_i R_j \subseteq R_{i+j}\)),
  so it lies in the degree-\((i+j)\) component. Since the quotient map is a ring
  homomorphism these conditions assemble into the
  \(\mathrm{GradedRing}\) structure on \(R/(x)\). This is the ring-level analogue
  of \(G_2\) and is the heaviest single block, as it bundles the decomposition with
  the graded-monoid multiplication data.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[$G_4$ -- regrading \(C\) and \(K\) over \(R/(x)\)]
  \label{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.regrade_over_quotient}
  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition,
    lem:graded_quotient_decomposition, lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}
  Both \(C = M/xM\) and \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) are annihilated by \(x\):
  \(x \cdot C = 0\) since \(xM \subseteq xM\), and \(x \cdot K = 0\) by definition
  of the kernel. Hence the \(R\)-action on each factors through the surjection
  \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\), and the component families of \(G_1\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}, for \(K_n\)) and \(G_2\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}, for \(C_n\)) carry a
  \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\) instance for the graded \(R/(x)\)-action of
  \cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}. Thus \(C\) and \(K\) are internally
  graded \(R/(x)\)-modules with the \emph{same} component families as over \(R\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition,
    lem:graded_quotient_decomposition, lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}
  Annihilation by \(x\) makes the \(R\)-module structures on \(C\) and \(K\) descend
  to \(R/(x)\)-module structures: for \(\bar r \in R/(x)\) the action
  \(\bar r \cdot (-)\) is well-defined because any two lifts of \(\bar r\) differ by
  an element of \((x)\), which annihilates \(C\) and \(K\). The \(\mathrm{DirectSum}\)
  decompositions of \(C\) and \(K\) are unchanged (same underlying additive
  families from \(G_1\), \(G_2\)). For the graded action, the residue
  \(\bar a \in (R/(x))_i\) (image of \(a \in R_i\)) acts on a degree-\(n\) component
  by \(\bar a \cdot c = a \cdot c\), which lands in component \(i+n\) by the
  \(R\)-graded action of \(G_1\)/\(G_2\); independence of the value from the lift
  \(a\) again follows from annihilation by \((x)\). This is exactly the
  \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\) instance for the \(R/(x)\)-grading of
  \cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[$G_5$ -- \(C\) and \(K\) are finitely generated over \(R/(x)\)]
  \label{lem:graded_finite_transfer}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finite_over_quotient}
  \uses{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient,
    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
  With \(R\) Noetherian, both \(C = M/xM\) and \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) are
  finitely generated graded \(R/(x)\)-modules. Together with the gradings of
  \(G_1\)--\(G_4\) this presents \(C\) and \(K\) as the finitely generated graded
  \(R/(x)\)-modules to which the inductive hypothesis of
  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} applies.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient,
    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
  As an \(R\)-module \(M\) is finitely generated; \(C = M/xM\) is a quotient of
  \(M\), hence finitely generated over \(R\), and \(K \subseteq M\) is a submodule
  of a finitely generated module over the Noetherian ring \(R\), hence also
  finitely generated over \(R\). By \(G_4\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}) both are annihilated by \((x)\), so
  their \(R\)-module structure factors through \(R \twoheadrightarrow R/(x)\).
  \cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}, applied to this surjection,
  transfers finite generation from \(R\) to \(R/(x)\): \(C\) and \(K\) are finitely
  generated as \(R/(x)\)-modules. Combined with the graded \(R/(x)\)-module
  structure of \(G_4\) they are finitely generated graded \(R/(x)\)-modules.
\end{proof}

\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}

\begin{lemma}[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
  \[
    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
  \]
  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), \(V = M_n\),
  \(W = M_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi = K_n\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) and
  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}, since the degree-\((n+1)\) part of
  \(xM\) is \(x M_n\)) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
  \[
    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
  \]
  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
  \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
  \(M_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = C_{n+1}\) through the
  gradings of \(G_1\) and \(G_2\), the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded
  piece of the homogeneous submodule \(xM\) is exactly \(x M_n\)
  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
\end{proof}

\emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
  degree-one generators, so that the induction hypothesis applies to finitely
  generated graded modules over \(R/(x)\), itself a graded quotient of
  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\). Multiplication by \(x\) raises degree by one
  and assembles, for every \(n\), into a short exact sequence of
  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector spaces
  \[
    0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{\;x\;} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0,
  \]
  where \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M / xM\) are finitely
  generated graded \(R\)-modules annihilated by \(x\), hence finitely generated
  graded modules over \(R/(x)\). By additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact
  sequence (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}, applied to the kernel and
  cokernel of the map \(x\)),
  \[
    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
  \]
  for every \(n\). Multiplying the Hilbert series of \(M\) by \((1 - X)\) and
  summing this identity against \(X^{n}\) shows that
  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) equals the Hilbert series of
  \(C\) minus that of \(K\), up to a polynomial in low degrees. By the induction
  hypothesis applied to the \(R/(x)\)-modules \(K\) and \(C\) (which live over
  \(r - 1\) variables), each of those Hilbert series is, up to a polynomial, of
  the form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\) with \(d - 1 \le r - 1\). Hence
  \[
    (1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
      \;=\; q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
  \]
  and multiplying by the inverse power series
  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1) = (1 - X)^{-1}\)
  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) gives
  \[
    \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
      \;=\; p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
  \]
  with \(d \le r\). Reading off the coefficient of \(X^n\) for \(n \gg 0\) yields
  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\),
  which is the claim.
\end{proof}

\emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
  degree-one generators. The quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is itself a graded quotient
  of \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\), hence an \((r-1)\)-generated graded
  \(\kappa\)-algebra to which the induction hypothesis applies; that \(R/(x)\) is a
  graded ring with degree-zero part \(\kappa\) is the content of \(G_3\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}), using that \((x)\) is homogeneous
  (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}).

  \emph{Manufacturing \(K\) and \(C\) as graded \(R/(x)\)-modules.} Put
  \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M/xM\). By \(G_1\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(K\) is an internally
  graded \(R\)-module with components \(K_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), and by
  \(G_2\) (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}) \(C = \mathrm{QuotSMulTop}\,
  x\,M\) is an internally graded \(R\)-module with components
  \(C_n = M_n/x M_{n-1}\). Both are annihilated by \(x\), so by \(G_4\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}) their action descends to \(R/(x)\) with
  the same component families, and by \(G_5\)
  (\cref{lem:graded_finite_transfer}) they are finitely generated graded
  \(R/(x)\)-modules (\(C\) a quotient of the finitely generated \(M\), \(K\) a
  submodule of \(M\) over the Noetherian \(R\)). Writing
  \(h_M(n) = \dim_\kappa M_n\), \(h_C(n) = \dim_\kappa C_n\),
  \(h_K(n) = \dim_\kappa K_n\), the induction hypothesis applied to the
  \(R/(x)\)-modules \(C\) and \(K\) yields a common order \(d\) (after
  \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} raises the lower of the two orders) with
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
  (\cref{def:ratHilb}).

  \emph{The difference identity.} Multiplication by \(x\) is the \(\kappa\)-linear
  map \(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}\); applying the degreewise rank--nullity identity
  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) with \(V = M_n\),
  \(W = M_{n+1}\) gives, for every \(n\),
  \[
    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
      \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n),
  \]
  using the identifications \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
  \(M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\) from \(G_1\), \(G_2\). This is exactly the
  \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis of the inductive-step engine.

  \emph{Conclusion.} Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\),
  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\) and the difference identity into
  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\): there are
  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d+1 \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d+1)}\bigr)\) for all
  \(n > N\), which is the claim (with pole order \(d+1 \le r\)).
\end{proof}
